messages.author: Zaos
messages.text: Companion relay 1.Relays public messages by zero hop repeating any flood messages it has heard 2.Relays Messages with specific companions as destination by zero hop repeating it. 3.Relays messages from specific companions to public flood by normal flood repeating it. 4.Relays messages from specific companions to its next path dest by flood. Use-case 1.Consistency of conversion - By having 1 companion you'll be able to see your entire convo history. you dont need to flip flop between multiple devices to see what's happening. 2.Range extension - By having a relay on the car, you can have it relay messages to your client thats in a much worse position such as in an office building or a store. This is without messing with peoples routings and reducing the airtime compared to if it was just a repeater. Why not Repeater? -Repeaters are meant to be stationary, too many moving repeaters cause extra traffic and messes with routing, while it is predicted to not cause a real issue on message reliability, it can still cause UX issues as message retries and flood to find a new path is timely. Why not Companion? -Companions dont share their messages, so if you have a companion you use daily, then get into your car, now you wont be able to message outside of the car efficiently. if you put a node ontop of your car, now you have to swap your app to your car companion, which breaks up conversations and ruins message history since now you wont be seeing previous messages, DM's now must also swap. i.e if "Jason" in this situation is messaging my client - "Zaos" and sends me a DM, but i actually have my zaos client in my pocket or left it at home since Im inside of my car driving, well now that message could entirely be lost, or i just wont see it since my car client is called "Zaos-Mobile" and its a different ID entirely. It is possible to shared the same key but i have not dived into that and it does not seem ideal security wise. (edited)
messages.content_html: Companion relay 1.Relays public messages by zero hop repeating any flood messages it has heard 2.Relays Messages with specific companions as destination by zero hop repeating it. 3.Relays messages from specific companions to public flood by normal flood repeating it. 4.Relays messages from specific companions to its next path dest by flood. Use-case 1.Consistency of conversion - By having 1 companion you'll be able to see your entire convo history. you dont need to flip flop between multiple devices to see what's happening. 2.Range extension - By having a relay on the car, you can have it relay messages to your client thats in a much worse position such as in an office building or a store. This is without messing with peoples routings and reducing the airtime compared to if it was just a repeater. Why not Repeater? -Repeaters are meant to be stationary, too many moving repeaters cause extra traffic and messes with routing, while it is predicted to not cause a real issue on message reliability, it can still cause UX issues as message retries and flood to find a new path is timely. Why not Companion? -Companions dont share their messages, so if you have a companion you use daily, then get into your car, now you wont be able to message outside of the car efficiently. if you put a node ontop of your car, now you have to swap your app to your car companion, which breaks up conversations and ruins message history since now you wont be seeing previous messages, DM's now must also swap. i.e if "Jason" in this situation is messaging my client - "Zaos" and sends me a DM, but i actually have my zaos client in my pocket or left it at home since Im inside of my car driving, well now that message could entirely be lost, or i just wont see it since my car client is called "Zaos-Mobile" and its a different ID entirely. It is possible to shared the same key but i have not dived into that and it does not seem ideal security wise. (edited)

messages.author: LeytonTheBiker
messages.text: The only different is MeshTastic floods every time, where as when a client gets ack it reports to the sender the path the message should take on the return. So the client sends direct... (Actually no different to a flood because spectrum occupancy is occupancy right ?) The message is picked up by a node with its hex, and ignored by the others. On the matching node re transmits until the message has arrived.

messages.author: stretch ZL3XL
messages.text: @Zaos people are saying "You're building MT" because what you're describing sounds awfully like MT's new "Zero-cost hops" feature. https://meshtastic.org/blog/zero-cost-hops-favorite-routers/ If your new relay role listens for messages FROM your companion only, and zero-hop repeats them, I guess no one beyond your companion and your relay are any the wiser. If your relay listens for messages TO your companion, and forwards them, and ignores any other messages, then it is indeed a relay and not a repeater.
messages.content_html: @Zaos people are saying "You're building MT" because what you're describing sounds awfully like MT's new "Zero-cost hops" feature. https://meshtastic.org/blog/zero-cost-hops-favorite-routers/ If your new relay role listens for messages FROM your companion only, and zero-hop repeats them, I guess no one beyond your companion and your relay are any the wiser. If your relay listens for messages TO your companion, and forwards them, and ignores any other messages, then it is indeed a relay and not a repeater.
embeds_html:
Zero-Cost Hops for Favorite Routers | Meshtastic
Stretch your mesh by stretching each hop by using zero-cost hops

messages.author: Zeb Palmer (IdahoMesh)
messages.text: Love the work you're doing here, it's very much needed. But please think seriously about how to name it. "Companion Relay"... I think that's going to confuse a lot of folks. Considering the primary use case for this behavior on MC is in a mobile environment, perhaps "Mobile Relay?" I think one of the fatal flaws of MT has been the prolifereation of roles and the fact that most people don't understand them. I love the simplicity of MC having two simple ones, but I very much believe we need something for this use case--with a good name. And I would very much focus on pitching this as a way to reduce the impact of mobile repeaters that already exist rather than adding a new role that will take up more air time. (edited)
messages.content_html: Love the work you're doing here, it's very much needed. But please think seriously about how to name it. "Companion Relay"... I think that's going to confuse a lot of folks. Considering the primary use case for this behavior on MC is in a mobile environment, perhaps "Mobile Relay?" I think one of the fatal flaws of MT has been the prolifereation of roles and the fact that most people don't understand them. I love the simplicity of MC having two simple ones, but I very much believe we need something for this use case--with a good name. And I would very much focus on pitching this as a way to reduce the impact of mobile repeaters that already exist rather than adding a new role that will take up more air time. (edited)

messages.author: bp-gone
messages.text: I haven't thought this all the way through, and it may not solve the "mobile repeater" problems... but avoiding adding new commands, could you just: 1) For FLOOD_GROUP_TEXT, if the path length is zero (first hop), act as a repeater (repeat as normal) 2) For FLOOD_GROUP_TEXT, if the path length is > 0, repeat it with zero hops. In #1, that packet could be from your companion in H-E-B, or it could be someone else nearby, and you aren't really hurting the mesh. Maybe even less so if your txdelay is set high (you are adding a hop and adding delay, so other repeaters nearby will ignore it?) Not sure of the other types of packets you might apply the same logic to...
messages.content_html: I haven't thought this all the way through, and it may not solve the "mobile repeater" problems... but avoiding adding new commands, could you just: 1) For FLOOD_GROUP_TEXT, if the path length is zero (first hop), act as a repeater (repeat as normal) 2) For FLOOD_GROUP_TEXT, if the path length is > 0, repeat it with zero hops. In #1, that packet could be from your companion in H-E-B, or it could be someone else nearby, and you aren't really hurting the mesh. Maybe even less so if your txdelay is set high (you are adding a hop and adding delay, so other repeaters nearby will ignore it?) Not sure of the other types of packets you might apply the same logic to...

Zaos
This means I only have 2 options now for flood messages 1. I just allow all flood messages from nearby clients, meaning I can use it, others can use it without an allowlist, kinda bordering normal repeater territory but the benefit is responses are zero hopped so it’s not like floods will continue to propagate from the relay 2. The only solution I could think of is to add two new commands, one command that adds the hashtag channels you communicate in like public and test etc, this populates the keys into the relay. And another command that adds contact names, this is the allowlist. So when a client sends a message and the path Len is 0 (meaning the relay heard it directly from client) it will decrypt the packet with its prepopulated key, check the name, if it matches the contact name in the new allowlist then it’ll forward the encrypted packet

messages.author: Zaos
messages.text: Companion relay 1.Relays public messages by zero hop repeating any flood messages it has heard 2.Relays Messages with specific companions as destination by zero hop repeating it. 3.Relays messages from specific companions to public flood by normal flood repeating it. 4.Relays messages from specific companions to its next path dest by flood. Use-case 1.Consistency of conversion - By having 1 companion you'll be able to see your entire convo history. you dont need to flip flop between multiple devices to see what's happening. 2.Range extension - By having a relay on the car, you can have it relay messages to your client thats in a much worse position such as in an office building or a store. This is without messing with peoples routings and reducing the airtime compared to if it was just a repeater. Why not Repeater? -Repeaters are meant to be stationary, too many moving repeaters cause extra traffic and messes with routing, while it is predicted to not cause a real issue on message reliability, it can still cause UX issues as message retries and flood to find a new path is timely. Why not Companion? -Companions dont share their messages, so if you have a companion you use daily, then get into your car, now you wont be able to message outside of the car efficiently. if you put a node ontop of your car, now you have to swap your app to your car companion, which breaks up conversations and ruins message history since now you wont be seeing previous messages, DM's now must also swap. i.e if "Jason" in this situation is messaging my client - "Zaos" and sends me a DM, but i actually have my zaos client in my pocket or left it at home since Im inside of my car driving, well now that message could entirely be lost, or i just wont see it since my car client is called "Zaos-Mobile" and its a different ID entirely. It is possible to shared the same key but i have not dived into that and it does not seem ideal security wise.^ (edited)
messages.content_html: Companion relay 1.Relays public messages by zero hop repeating any flood messages it has heard 2.Relays Messages with specific companions as destination by zero hop repeating it. 3.Relays messages from specific companions to public flood by normal flood repeating it. 4.Relays messages from specific companions to its next path dest by flood. Use-case 1.Consistency of conversion - By having 1 companion you'll be able to see your entire convo history. you dont need to flip flop between multiple devices to see what's happening. 2.Range extension - By having a relay on the car, you can have it relay messages to your client thats in a much worse position such as in an office building or a store. This is without messing with peoples routings and reducing the airtime compared to if it was just a repeater. Why not Repeater? -Repeaters are meant to be stationary, too many moving repeaters cause extra traffic and messes with routing, while it is predicted to not cause a real issue on message reliability, it can still cause UX issues as message retries and flood to find a new path is timely. Why not Companion? -Companions dont share their messages, so if you have a companion you use daily, then get into your car, now you wont be able to message outside of the car efficiently. if you put a node ontop of your car, now you have to swap your app to your car companion, which breaks up conversations and ruins message history since now you wont be seeing previous messages, DM's now must also swap. i.e if "Jason" in this situation is messaging my client - "Zaos" and sends me a DM, but i actually have my zaos client in my pocket or left it at home since Im inside of my car driving, well now that message could entirely be lost, or i just wont see it since my car client is called "Zaos-Mobile" and its a different ID entirely. It is possible to shared the same key but i have not dived into that and it does not seem ideal security wise.^ (edited)

messages.author: PogiKwanKenobe
messages.text: I'm finding this does kinda make it hard to troubleshoot effectively. Hard to tell if I'm not really being heard, or if at the time I'm sending things others just aren't actively checking their app messages. I haven't really received many messages while out and about and testing, so far. (I did add the companion relay pub key, but didn't realize for the first few days that that had to be set. Helps to read the readme!)

messages.author: bp-gone
messages.text: I think you are referring to the companion repeater mode which is the opposite of what @Zaos is working on. The core software has introduced a mode where companions can repeat (with restrictions). This thread/project is a repeater that is restricted to assisting a companion, and is not (yet?) part of MeshCore. But is interoperable (and maybe someday part of MeshCore repo maybe?) (edited)
messages.content_html: I think you are referring to the companion repeater mode which is the opposite of what @Zaos is working on. The core software has introduced a mode where companions can repeat (with restrictions). This thread/project is a repeater that is restricted to assisting a companion, and is not (yet?) part of MeshCore. But is interoperable (and maybe someday part of MeshCore repo maybe?) (edited)

messages.author: bp-gone
messages.text: It looks like there are a handful of files that were munged a bit, and show up as diffs, but a quick browse indicates that they aren't different for most of the shown diffs (formatting diffs). I think a good goal would be to start clean, but hand import your differences again. What's best to do is do a fresh "git clone" of the upstream repo, then create a new branch, then add your edits. I'm not a git expert either, so that's about all I have for you Thank you, I'm going to take a look at it and maybe attempt what I just described above.
